title: Syntax & Types
primer:
Every shading language shares the same mathematical heart — vectors, matrices, and scalar arithmetic — but each spells those ideas differently. GLSL uses vec2/vec3/vec4; HLSL uses float2/float3/float4; Slang follows HLSL style but adds modern language features on top.

Beyond type names, two common operations trip up language switchers: interpolation (mix in GLSL, lerp in HLSL/Slang) and swizzles — the shorthand that lets you reorder or select vector components. Swizzles work identically across all three languages, but the coordinate-set letter choices can differ in meaning by convention.

In this lesson you will translate small HLSL snippets to idiomatic GLSL ES 3.0, fixing type names and function calls as you go. The goal is pattern recognition: once you can sight-read the rename table, cross-language porting becomes mechanical.

challenges.title: float3 → vec3 and lerp → mix
hints: HLSL's float3 maps directly to GLSL's vec3 — same four-letter pattern, different prefix.
HLSL's lerp(a, b, t) is GLSL's mix(a, b, t) — same argument order, different name.
challenges.unlocks_theory: GLSL and HLSL share identical vector math semantics; only the spelling differs. The rename table is: float2→vec2, float3→vec3, float4→vec4, lerp→mix, saturate→clamp(x,0,1), frac→fract. Swizzles (.xyz, .rgb, .xy) work identically in both languages.

hlsl.note: Original HLSL snippet the user is translating FROM.

slang.note: Slang follows HLSL naming; lerp is also available as mix via the standard library.

challenges.title: Swizzles and constructors
challenges.description:
Swizzles are one of the most powerful (and syntax-identical) features across shader languages. The HLSL below uses swizzles and a 4→3 constructor chain. Translate it to GLSL.

HLSL reference:
```hlsl
float2 uv = float2(0.3, 0.7);
float4 color = float4(uv, 0.0, 1.0);  // (r=0.3, g=0.7, b=0, a=1)
float3 rgb   = color.rgb;              // swizzle: take first 3 components
float3 bgr   = color.bgr;             // swizzle: reverse to blue-green-red
// output bgr as the final color
```
hints: Swizzle syntax (.rgb, .bgr, .xy, .zw) is identical in GLSL and HLSL — no change needed there.
Only the type names change: float2→vec2, float4→vec4, float3→vec3.
challenges.unlocks_theory: Swizzles are syntactic sugar built into all major shading languages and compile to zero-cost register reordering on the GPU. The letter sets .xyzw (geometry) and .rgba (color) are interchangeable in both GLSL and HLSL — .r and .x refer to the same component. Slang inherits this from HLSL unchanged.

hlsl.note: Original HLSL — swizzle syntax is identical, only constructor type names differ.

slang.note: Slang follows HLSL swizzle rules exactly.

challenges.title: Animated gradient with type fixes
hints: Count the broken identifiers: float2→vec2, float3→vec3, float4→vec4. That covers all the type errors.
lerp(a, b, t) becomes mix(a, b, t) in GLSL — same arguments, different name.
challenges.unlocks_theory: When porting HLSL to GLSL, a global find-and-replace handles most type names: float2/3/4→vec2/3/4, matrix float4x4→mat4, lerp→mix, saturate(x)→clamp(x,0.0,1.0), frac→fract, ddx/ddy→dFdx/dFdy. Semantic annotations like : SV_Target and : SV_Position have no GLSL equivalent and are simply removed.

hlsl.note: Correct HLSL version of the animated gradient.

slang.note: Slang can use lerp or mix; here we use lerp to match HLSL style.
